Abstract -------- Wearable thermoelectric devices provide a favorable solution for wearable electronics, due to their ability to harvest electricity or regulate human body temperature. To this end, there have been extensive studies of thermoelectric devices for wearable applications, employing diverse materials, structures, designs, and other possible techniques to improve performance. In this paper, we present a comprehensive review of recent wearable thermoelectric device works that examine how the materials and design geometry properties can influence the key performance parameters, along with potential real-life applications. The review mainly discusses three main factors of materials, geometry, and configuration modules, along with the strengths and weaknesses of the corresponding materials and designs. Finally, we evaluate the prominent wearable applications based on the diverse materials, structures, and module design requirements, along with future outlook and potential opportunities for next generation thermoelectric wearables. Introduction ------------ The market size of thermoelectric generators (TEGs) was assessed to be USD $799 million in 2022 and is anticipated to reach USD $1633.76 million by 2030, with a cumulative average gross rate of 9.7% projected over the forecast period (2023−2030). [1] The increasing need for TEGs in the healthcare, automotive, transportation, and industrial sectors has contributed to the growth of the market, driven by the energy harvesting capability and minimal maintenance requirements of TEGs. Energy harvesting technologies are expected to play a vital role in wearable electronics because energy harvesting serves to reduce dependency on the onboard batteries of wearable electronics. Thus, wearable thermoelectric devices (WTEDS) can offer a viable solution as a promising candidate for self-powering wearable electronics with the human body temperature to supplement or replace onboard rigid batteries, as WTEDs can use the human body temperature to self-power other wearable devices. [2,3] However, the primary challenge associated with WTEDs lies in their limited output, which is mainly due to (i) the limited thermoelectric conversion efficiency, and (ii) the narrow temperature difference between the human body and external surroundings. The factors affecting these primary challenges include material properties, the Seebeck coefficient, and heat dissipation issues. Consequently, an in-depth examination of thermoelectric materials, structures, and configurational design to enhance the output of WTEDs needs to be conducted. For instance, to overcome these challenges investigation has been performed to analyze properties (structural, electronic, mechanical, thermoelectric, and optical), material design, and structure design. [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8]] While multiple reviews have reported thermoelectric devices for wearable applications, for instance, Z. Soleimani et al. reviewed the output voltage and power of wearable thermoelectric generators, considering various geometries and material compositions. P. Wu et al. provided a comprehensive overview of flexible thermoelectric materials, device configuration, substrate selection, and fabrication techniques, highlighting recent research trends in the field. A. Nozariasbmarz et al. focused on the physiological aspects of the human body involved in heat generation and analyzed system requirements along with the impact of different parameters on thermoelectric generator performance. [[9], [10], [11], [12], [13], [14], [15], [16]] According to the best of our knowledge, no review has comprehensively compared in a comprehensive manner the key performance parameters of WTEDs based on the (i) types of material used to comprise the thermoelectric legs, (ii) structural design, (iii) configural design, and (iv) thermal management techniques. Here, the structural design describes the types of physical structures that the thermoelectric materials form, including thin film, fiber, and dice types. Additionally, the configural design pertains to the arrangement of these structurally designed thermoelectric materials to comprise the thermoelectric device, such as π, S, Y, and zigzag configurations. Lastly, the thermal management techniques of the device refer to heat sinks that dissipate heat to maintain a temperature gradient between both sides, these techniques being mainly comprised of metallic, foam, or phase change materials (PCMs), and interfacial techniques. In this paper, we systematically review the WTEDs based on innovative strategies to improve the output performance of thermoelectric devices for wearable applications, with particular emphasis on the materials and geometric designs of thermoelectric devices. To present the review in systematic order, this article introduces the performance parameters that affect the overall performance of WTEDs, followed by the types of materials, structural design of materials, configural design of the device, and thermal management techniques, as shown in Fig. 1. Then, we examine the real-life applications of WTEDs, and conclude with the outlook and commercial challenges, highlighting the potential opportunities for the imminent commercialization of WTEDs. These values correspond to an increase in viscosity from 223 mPa·s to 559 mPa·s, confirming the effect of viscosity on both orientation degree and interfacial thermal resistance. This approach also provides a promising new method for detailed ITR determination in well-dispersed thermal conductive materials. [PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) PMCID: PMC9230808 PMID: [35744374](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35744374/) Abstract -------- Recently, self-powered health monitoring systems using a wearable thermoelectric generator (WTEG) have been rapidly developed since no battery is needed for continuous signal monitoring, and there is no need to worry about battery leakage. However, the existing materials and devices have limitations in rigid form factors and small-scale manufacturing. Moreover, the conventional bulky WTEG is not compatible with soft and deformable tissues, including human skins or internal organs. These limitations restrict the WTEG from stabilizing the thermoelectric gradient that is necessary to harvest the maximum body heat and generate valuable electrical energy. This paper summarizes recent advances in soft, flexible materials and device designs to overcome the existing challenges. Specifically, we discuss various organic and inorganic thermoelectric materials with their properties for manufacturing flexible devices. In addition, this review discusses energy budgets required for effective integration of WTEGs with wearable biomedical systems, which is the main contribution of this article compared to previous articles. Lastly, the key challenges of the existing WTEGs are discussed, followed by describing future perspectives for self-powered health monitoring systems. **Keywords:** thermoelectric material, energy harvesting, nanogenerator, wearable device, self-powered system 1. Introduction --------------- Self-powered health monitoring systems are employed mainly to track the health vitals of players during exercise and training and for the regular health monitoring of elders. These systems are usually powered by batteries charged either by a conventional power source or by wearable energy harvesting devices, i.e., ferroelectric, thermoelectric, triboelectric, and piezoelectric [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B1-materials-15-04315)]. Currently, our lives have been dramatically changed with the emergence of these wearable health monitoring systems. As a result, the advent of the internet of things, “IoT,” has increased the energy demand for wearable devices [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B2-materials-15-04315)]. Furthermore, the purchase of personalized clothing and smartwatches has increased from 2.6% to 8.19% and 24.48% to 30.97% in 2021, respectively, as shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f001)A [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B2-materials-15-04315)]. This increase in wearables’ adaptability has resulted in exploring freely and readily available renewable energy resources to meet the energy demand without carbon footprint [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B3-materials-15-04315),[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B4-materials-15-04315)]. Energy harvesting systems, such as ferroelectric, thermoelectric, triboelectric, and piezoelectric, harvest natural forms of energy, i.e., biomechanical, thermal, mechanical, and vibrational energies, to power the wearables, respectively [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B5-materials-15-04315),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B6-materials-15-04315),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B7-materials-15-04315)]. ### Figure 1. [![Image 9: Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cd50/9230808/998ee0370090/materials-15-04315-g001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=9230808_materials-15-04315-g001.jpg) [Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/figure/materials-15-04315-f001/) (**A**) The market share of wearable electronics in the global market between 2018 and 2021; reprinted from Ref. [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B2-materials-15-04315)]. Copyright year (2020), Nozariasbmarz, A.; Collins, H.; Dsouza, K.; Polash, M.H.; Hosseini, M.; Hyland, M.; Liu, J.; Malhotra, A.; Ortiz, F.M.; Mohaddes, F.; et al. with permission from Elsevier; (**B**) The comparative analysis of the maximum reported output power generated by piezoelectric, thermoelectric, and triboelectric [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B9-materials-15-04315),[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B10-materials-15-04315),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B21-materials-15-04315)]. (**C**–**E**) The comparative analysis of electrical conductivities, Seebeck coefficient, and ZT of organic, inorganic, hybrid matrix, and polymer at room temperature for WTEG application, respectively [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B18-materials-15-04315),[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B19-materials-15-04315),[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B20-materials-15-04315),[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B22-materials-15-04315),[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B23-materials-15-04315)]. (The ZT value of Ag-BiSbTe was not given, and it cannot be calculated because thermal conductivity of film was not reported in the article—[[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B23-materials-15-04315)]). A ferroelectric generator works on the principle of the direct piezoelectric effect and can be piezoelectric and pyroelectric at the same time. It uses spontaneous electric polarization, which can be actuated by a change in stress or temperature, resulting in the considerable potential to power a biosensing system [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B7-materials-15-04315)]. A piezoelectric generator converts human biomechanical energy into electrical energy by using the piezoelectric effect and generates a voltage of up to 55 V when force is exerted by the human foot [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B5-materials-15-04315)]. Such a high voltage generation increases the scope of piezoelectric to power biosensing devices. However, the abrupt output power at low frequency limits its real-time application to biosensors [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B8-materials-15-04315),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B9-materials-15-04315)]. In addition, it requires an active state to harvest biomechanical energy. A triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) harvests mechanical energy during surface charge transfer between two oppositely charged surfaces at a reasonable distance [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B10-materials-15-04315)]. TENG has a wide area of applications and has successfully worked as a self-powered pressure sensor [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B11-materials-15-04315)]. Using the Seebeck effect, a TEG harvests human body heat and converts it directly into electrical energy. In recent years, TEG has successfully powered various biosensors at a lab-scale [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B12-materials-15-04315)]. The key advantage of these energy harvesting systems is their multifunctionality [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B11-materials-15-04315)]. Along with energy harvesting, piezoelectric works to detect human motion as a strain sensor, TENG works as a pressure sensor, and TEG works as a temperature sensor. A comparative analysis of output power generated from TEG, TENG, and piezoelectric is given in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f001)B, which shows that the thermoelectric system has a slightly lower output power compared to the triboelectric system. However, TEGs are capable of harvesting human body heat in any state, i.e., at rest or during work, without extra effort. Moreover, TEGs offer no-cost cooling to smart electronics, which is an added feature of this renewable energy harvesting system [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B13-materials-15-04315),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B14-materials-15-04315)]. A thermoelectric generator (TEG) is a solid-state device built on a semiconducting material. It directly converts thermal energy into electrical energy by using the Seebeck effect. Recently, there has been considerable improvement in the thermoelectric properties, i.e., the electrical conductivity (), Seebeck coefficient (S), and figure of merit (ZT), of thermoelectric (TE) materials [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B13-materials-15-04315)]. Highly sophisticated equipment is used to measure these TE properties. The four-point probe and laser flash method measure the electrical conductivity and the thermal conductivity of TEG, respectively [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B15-materials-15-04315),[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B16-materials-15-04315)]. The Seebeck coefficient is measured by dividing the difference in voltage at room temperature and required temperature by the difference in temperatures (S = −ΔV/ΔT). Commercial equipment, such as the ULVAC ZEM-3 and thin-film analyzer, is used to measure Seebeck coefficients and TE power [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B16-materials-15-04315),[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B17-materials-15-04315)]. [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f001)C–E show a comparative analysis of electrical conductivities, Seebeck coefficient, and ZT for frequently reported thermoelectric materials. It can be seen from [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f001)C that the electrical conductivity varies from 10 3 to 10 5 S/m for TE materials. A recent study reported an electrical conductivity of 3.63 × 10 5 S/m for n-type SWCNT film, which is a huge development for TEs [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B18-materials-15-04315)]. However, the Seebeck coefficient is higher for inorganic materials, i.e., ~187 µV/K for bulk BiTe alloy [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B19-materials-15-04315)] and −96 µV/K for TiS 2 [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B20-materials-15-04315)], which directly increases the ZT as shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f001)D,E. Considerable efforts are necessary to improve the Seebeck coefficient of SWCNTs and polymer materials. This is because the SWCNT-based films have a thickness of a few microns and exhibited a planar architecture that impacts the generation of voltage at both ends. In recent studies, the focus has been to address the challenges of these highly electrically conductive materials, and innovative designs are being investigated. In [Section 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#sec4-materials-15-04315) and [Section 5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#sec5-materials-15-04315), we will discuss additional details and proposed future directions to address the aforementioned challenges. [Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f002) shows the advancement in the design and soft packaging of WTEG for flexibility and stretchability [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B15-materials-15-04315),[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B18-materials-15-04315),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B21-materials-15-04315),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B24-materials-15-04315),[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B25-materials-15-04315),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B26-materials-15-04315)]. It indicates considerable improvements in the thermoelectric performance of flexible TEGs [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B18-materials-15-04315),[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B19-materials-15-04315),[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B22-materials-15-04315),[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B23-materials-15-04315)]. It is noteworthy that soft packaging improved the strain tolerance from 3% to 20% for inorganic thermoelectric material-based flexible TEGs, which successfully generated thermoelectric power from ~693.5 nW (13 np pairs, ΔT = 24 K) to ~7.02 mW (220 np pairs, ΔT = 40 K) [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B15-materials-15-04315),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B21-materials-15-04315)]. Although organic material-based TEG devices successfully generated ~39 µW (336 np pairs, ΔT = 50 K), however, these could only generate 2.6 µW at ΔT = 6 K for wearable applications, which is sufficient to power a low-power biosensor with a good strain tolerance [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B24-materials-15-04315)]. Another parameter, magnetothermopower, to improve the thermoelectric performance of TEGs has been introduced. This parameter has a strong relationship with the Seebeck coefficient (S) in magnetic semiconducting material [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B27-materials-15-04315)]. It is reported that an external magnetic field has a strong impact on the electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and Seebeck coefficient. In ferromagnetic semiconducting material, ferrons exist at the Curie temperature and fall quickly as the outside magnetic field changes at T > room temperature (RT). This fall of ferrons generates a large potential difference, which eventually improves the ZT of the TEG [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B28-materials-15-04315)]. Further exploration of its impact on the performance of TEG is required for magnetic semiconducting TE materials. However, this review only focuses on nonmagnetic semiconducting materials. ### Figure 2. (**A**) SWCNT-based TEG, reprinted from [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B18-materials-15-04315)], copyright (2017), Zhou, W.; Fan, Q.; Zhang, Q.; Cai, L.; Li, K.; Gu, X.; Yang, F.; Zhang, N.; Wang, Y.; Liu, H.; et al. with permission from nature communications; (**B**) flexible Bi 2 Te 3 film, reprinted from [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B15-materials-15-04315)], copyright (2019), Kong, D.; Zhu, W.; Guo, Z.; Deng, Y. with permission from Elsevier; (**C**) Bi 2 Te 3, silver-coated nickel (Ag–Ni), Ag nanowire-based TEG, reprinted from [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B21-materials-15-04315)] copyright (2020), Lee, B.; Cho, H.; Park, K.T.; Kim, J.-S.; Park, M.; Kim, H.; Hong, Y.; Chung, S., with permission from Nature communications; (**D**) flexible SWCNTs TEG, reprinted from [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B24-materials-15-04315)], copyright (2021), Mytafides, C.K.; Tzounis, L.; Karalis, G.; Formanek, P.; Paipetis, A.S. with permission from Elsevier; (**E**) copper foil, n-type BiTeSe, and p-type BiSbTe alloys, reprinted from [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B25-materials-15-04315)]; (**F**) SWCNT-based e-skin TEG, reprinted from [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B26-materials-15-04315)], copyright (2021), Kim, M.H.; Cho, C.H.; Kim, J.S.; Nam, T.U.; Kim, W.-S.; Lee, T.I.; Oh, J.Y. with permission from Elsevier. Thermoelectric generator-powered wearable health monitoring sensors utilize body heat to monitor health vitals. TEG-powered biosensors require mechanical robustness, flexibility, and stretchability to overcome the strain effect during the working state. Despite the advances in biosensors, the integration of WTEGs with biosensors is difficult. Currently, the research focus of WTEGs has shifted to film-based WTEGs to achieve active integration. It is necessary for WTEGs to be in good conformity with the human body, which ensures the maximum heat conduction through interfacial layers and stabilizes the thermal gradient. A TEG-based biosensing system is comprised of six main components: (1) TEG, (2) integrated power management system (IPMS), (3) interconnects, (4) biosensor, (5) actuator, and (6) transmission system [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B12-materials-15-04315)]. For powering the biosensing system, the WTEG must be well adhered to the human skin to generate enough voltage to not only derive the IPMS but also power the biosensor with a good strain tolerance [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B21-materials-15-04315)]. Currently, the WTEGs built on TE legs have a low strain tolerance, hence the trends in the fabrication of WTEGs shifting to film-based TEGs to offer good interfacial strength and compatibility with biosensing devices [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B17-materials-15-04315),[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B29-materials-15-04315),[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B30-materials-15-04315),[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B31-materials-15-04315)]. A detailed discussion is provided in [Section 7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#sec7-materials-15-04315), [Section 8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#sec8-materials-15-04315) and [Section 9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#sec9-materials-15-04315). We formulated this review by first discussing the challenging aspects of thermoelectric materials for WTEG and their performance and suggesting measures to improve the TE properties. Then, the designs and packaging methods of WTEGs are reviewed and future recommendations are proposed. Lastly, energy system analysis is performed to effectively integrate the WTEG with a wearable health monitoring system by reducing the overload energy budget. Future directions are given for advancing the WTEGs. 2. Why Thermoelectrics? ----------------------- In a recent annual energy outlook report of EIA USA, a sharp surge in the energy demand has been projected, and a 30% rise in energy consumption from 2020 to 2050 has been predicted [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B32-materials-15-04315),[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B33-materials-15-04315)]. This increase in energy consumption is somehow related to the emergence and adaptability of electronics, especially wearable electronics for personal use [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B34-materials-15-04315)]. In recent years, this increase in energy demand has anticipated the search for new ways to power wearable electronics and the exploration of renewable energy resources, i.e., photovoltaics (PVs) and thermoelectric (TE), as power sources for wearable electronic devices. The energy generation from PVs and thermoelectrics has several advantages, i.e., a direct, immediate, and environmentally friendly power supply; however, energy harvesting from PVs has a few disadvantages. It is a fact that solar energy is only available in the daytime, which causes its limited availability and restricts its application to wearable technology, especially for active tracking and health monitoring devices in off-hours. Thermoelectric has the potential to power wearables without interruption during off-hours. A typical fabrication process for a WTEG includes four simple steps: 1—fabrication of bottom and top electrodes; 2—fabrication of p- and n-type legs; 3—placement of n- and p-type legs over electrodes and soldering them; and 4—soft packaging of the WTEG. It exhibits the advantage of converting human body heat into electrical energy at very low-temperature differences, i.e., ΔT = ~3 K, by using the Seebeck effect of thermoelectric material and generates electrical energy from a few nW to several mW [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B19-materials-15-04315),[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B22-materials-15-04315),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B24-materials-15-04315),[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B35-materials-15-04315)] ([Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f003)A). It is advantageous because the human thermoregulatory system continuously regulates the human body temperature to 37 °C ([Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f003)B). However, its energy conversion efficiency has yet to improve [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B36-materials-15-04315),[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B37-materials-15-04315),[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B38-materials-15-04315)]. A WTEG is a solid-state device made up of semiconducting material. It comprises n- and p-type pairs connected electrically in series and thermally in parallel. It converts thermal energy into electrical energy by using the Seebeck effect. The Seebeck effect generates a voltage potential between hot and cold sides when a temperature gradient () is established across the WTEG ([Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#materials-15-04315-f003)C) and measured in terms of the Seebeck coefficient () [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B26-materials-15-04315),[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B34-materials-15-04315),[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B39-materials-15-04315),[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B40-materials-15-04315),[41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B41-materials-15-04315)]. In TE materials, the energy states of the electrons and holes establish the energy transfer inside the TE legs, which is measured by the Fermi–Dirac distribution. The Fermi–Dirac distribution was invented by Fermi and Dirac in 1926 to estimate the number of active electrons [[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B42-materials-15-04315),[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B43-materials-15-04315)]. (The Reference [[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9230808/#B42-materials-15-04315)] of the Fermi–Dirac distribution is the translated version of the original article written by Fermi, which is not found on the internet. This version was translated by the A. Zannoni Institute for Physical Science and Technology, University of Maryland at College Park, College Park, Maryland 20742 and the Electron and Optical Physics Division, National Institute of Standards and Technology, Gaithersburg, Maryland 20899). It is used to calculate the energy densities of the electrons responsible for current flow in the state of thermodynamic equilibrium. * 37 Citations * 1 Altmetric * [Metrics details](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1/metrics) Abstract -------- Wearable thermoelectric generators provide a reliable power generation method for self-powered wearable electronic devices. However, there has been a lack of research regarding the comfort of wearable thermoelectric generators. Here we propose a design for a comfortable wearable thermoelectric generators system with high output power based on sandwiched thermoelectric model. This model paves the way for simultaneously optimizing comfort (skin temperature and pressure perception) and output power by systematically considering a variety of thermal resistive environments and bending states, the properties of the thermoelectric and encapsulation materials, and the device structure. To verify this strategy, we fabricate wearable thermoelectric generators using Mg-based thermoelectric materials. These materials have great potential for replacing traditional Bi 2 Te 3-based materials and enable our wearable thermoelectric generators with a power density of 18.4 μWcm−2 under a wearing pressure of 0.8 kPa and with a skin temperature of 33 °C, ensuring the wearer’s comfort. ### Similar content being viewed by others ![Image 3](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41378-023-00583-3/MediaObjects/41378_2023_583_Figa_HTML.png) ### [Flexible thermoelectric generator and energy management electronics powered by body heat](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41378-023-00583-3?fromPaywallRec=false) Article Open access 24 August 2023 ![Image 4](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-020-19756-z/MediaObjects/41467_2020_19756_Fig1_HTML.png) ### [High-performance compliant thermoelectric generators with magnetically self-assembled soft heat conductors for self-powered wearable electronics](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-19756-z?fromPaywallRec=false) Article Open access 23 November 2020 ![Image 5](https://media.springernature.com/w215h120/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-025-57889-1/MediaObjects/41467_2025_57889_Fig1_HTML.png) ### [Three-dimensional flexible thermoelectric fabrics for smart wearables](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57889-1?fromPaywallRec=false) Article Open access 14 March 2025 Introduction ------------ Rapid progress in personalization propelled by remarkable advancements in wearable and implantable electronics is revolutionizing the field of healthcare[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR1 "Gao, W., Ota, H., Kiriya, D., Takei, K. & Javey, A. Hence, achieving comfort and high output simultaneously is not straightforward. In essence, maximizing output does not rely only on optimizing TE material performance, but also requires meticulous design of the w-TEG’s structure and thermal management at the system level. However, no general analytical formula for system efficiency and wearability of w-TEGs has been reported to date. In this study, we establish a model for w-TEGs utilizing a sandwiched structure, the most representative of TE devices. By integrating mechanical and TE analyses, we derive explicit formulas to quantitatively analyze the optimization of multidimensional parameters for both comfort and power output while worn on a human body in real-world environments. We establish a direct link between the system’s output and its wearing comfort by considering the interconnections among the structural parameters of the device, the performance of the TE materials, and the external environment. We identify the optimal structural matching range for maximizing system efficiency and wearability and predict the maximum output power of w-TEGs positioned at various locations on or near the human body. Moreover, experimental validation is conducted on actual human subjects using w-TEGs that employ n-type Mg 3 Bi 2 and _p_-type MgAgSb-based TE materials (_n_-type: Mg 3.2 Bi 1.49 Sb 0.5 Se 0.01; p-type: MgAg 0.95 Sb 0.99; the TE performance of each is shown in Supplementary Fig. S[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#MOESM1)). Recently, these materials have been intensively studied for use in non-wearable TEGs due to their cost effectiveness and high TE performance near room temperature[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR24 "Liu, Z. et al. Demonstration of ultrahigh thermoelectric efficiency of ∼7.3% in Mg3Sb2/MgAgSb module for low-temperature energy harvesting. Joule 5, 1196–1208 (2021)."),[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR25 "Liang, J. S. et al. Synergistic effect of band and nanostructure engineering on the boosted thermoelectric performance of n‐Type Mg3+δ (Sb, Bi)2Zintls. Adv. Energy Mater. 12, 202201086 (2022)."). The outcomes here closely approximate those for state-of-the-art Bi 2 Te 3-based w-TEGs, highlighting the potential of our fabricated w-TEGs. Our work provides a comprehensive strategy for the design of w-TEG systems, incorporating a multidimensional quantification analysis to maximize device output power while ensuring wearing comfort. Energy 35, 602–610 (2010)."),[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR29 "Eldesoukey, A. & Hassan, H. 3D model of thermoelectric generator (TEG) case study: effect of flow regime on the TEG performance. Energy Convers. Manag. 180, 231–239 (2019)."). Nevertheless, its computational cost is notably high, and it cannot offer concise and profound guidance similar to that from analytical formulas, making it challenging to analyze coupling mechanisms and quantify parameters from a macroscopic perspective. One-dimensional (1D) numerical analysis is straightforward and efficient[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR30 "Zhu, K. et al. System efficiency and power: the bridge between the device and system of a thermoelectric power generator. Energy Environ. Sci. 13, 3514–3526 (2020)."),[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR31 "Kishore, R. A., Nozariasbmarz, A., Poudel, B., Sanghadasa, M. & Priya, S. Ultra-high performance wearable thermoelectric coolers with less materials. Nat. Commun. 10, 1765 (2019)."). It is often employed for general, macroscopic mechanism quantification, aiding in parameter guidance for w-TEG design. Here we have proposed a complete design strategy based on the 1D coupling mechanism. Figure[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#Fig1) illustrates the logical framework for the full parameter optimization of a wearable TE power generation module based on the coupled field equations of TE and mechanical models. Our design strategy takes into account various application scenarios by considering environmental temperature (_T_ _a_), bending radius (\(r\)), body temperature (\({T}_{b}\)), and parameters of the TE and encapsulation materials, including Young’s modulus (_E_) and thermal conductivity (\({\kappa }_{E}\)) values for the encapsulation materials. Here the encapsulation materials are defined as the filler and substrates of the w-TEG. For a given environment and set of materials, the output and wearability of the system are highly dependent on the TE leg geometry and the heat dissipation design[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR32 "Xing, Y. et al. High-performance wearable Bi2Te3-based thermoelectric generator. Appl. Sci. 13, 5971 (2023)."),[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR33 "Lee, Y. G. et al. Design and experimental investigation of thermoelectric generators for wearable applications. Adv. Mater. Technol. 2, 1600292 (2017)."),[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#ref-CR34 "Leonov, V. & Vullers, R. J. M. Wearable thermoelectric generators for body-powered devices. J. Electron. Mater. 38, 1491–1498 (2009)."). We first established the connection between the wearability parameters for the system, including skin pressure (\(\Pr\)) and skin temperature (\({T}_{s}\)), and those for its structure, including length (\(L\))/width (\(w\)) of the TE leg, the fill factor (\(F\)), the _p_-/_n_-type material area ratio (\({F}_{p/n}\)), the ambient and total heat transfer coefficient (\({h}_{a},h\)), and obtained the ranges of values for the structural parameters. Then, on the base of ensuring wearability, the optimal geometric parameters of the TE leg were obtained by combining multiple parameters and optimizing the output efficiency of the system. When determining optimal geometric parameters that meet the design requirements, the properties of the TE and encapsulation materials must also be properly considered since, on the one hand, the TE materials directly determine the electrical and thermal performance of the w-TEG system under ideal conditions, while on the other hand, its mechanical performance mainly depends on the physical properties of the encapsulation materials. For example, using low-_E_ filling materials for the TE legs or the absence of filling materials can greatly improve the flexibility of the device but reduce its reliability. The device design should thus be based on the application environment and incorporate matching encapsulation materials. After the optimal geometric parameters were obtained, FEA was used for more precise adjustment to maximize the power output while meeting the wearing resistance requirements for any given material and environmental conditions and the device was finally prepared for experimental verification. **Fig. 1: Logical framework for the full-parameter optimization of a w-TEG.** [![Image 6: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-52841-1/MediaObjects/41467_2024_52841_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1/figures/1) The target parameters (\({V}_{d},\)\({P}_{d},\)\(\Pr,\)\({T}_{s},\)\({R}_{l}\)) are determined by the application environment, the optimized process, and optimal geometric parameters for w-TEG preparation and performance verification. [Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1/figures/1) ### Coupling mechanism between output performance and wearing comfort To assess the various aspects of w-TEG system performance, we have separately constructed thermal-electric and mechanical models of the device when worn on a human body as ideally simulated (no displacement between the two materials, without considering the centerline stretching and without considering Poisson’s ratio, etc.). The thermal-electric model primarily focuses on investigating the w-TEG’s output performance and \({T}_{s}\), while the mechanical model is employed to study the flexibility of the w-TEG and determine the minimum pressure exerted on the skin when worn. The mechanical model is shown in Fig.[2a, b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#Fig2) and assumes a cylindrical shape for the body part on which the w-TEG is worn. According to static equilibrium principles, given its three-layer structure, the bending stiffness (\({{Ei}}_{{TEG}}\)) of the system should satisfy the following expression (see Supplementary Note S[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#MOESM1)): $${{Ei}}_{{TEG}}={E}_{b}^{ * }{i}_{b}+{E}^{ * }i+{E}_{t}^{ * }{i}_{t}$$ (1) **Fig. 2: w-TEG models.** [![Image 7: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-024-52841-1/MediaObjects/41467_2024_52841_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1/figures/2) **a** Schematic illustration of the internal structure of the w-TEG. **b** Mechanical model of the w-TEG during bending. **c** Thermal network of the w-TEG system. [Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1/figures/2) In this context, \({E}_{b}^{ * },\)\({E}^{ * },\) and \({E}_{t}^{ * }\) represent the effective Young’s modulus of the bottom substrate (hot side of TEG), the filler layer, and the top substrate (cold side of TEG), respectively, and are functions of the material’s Young’s modulus and \(F\). \({i}_{b},\)\(i,\) and \({i}_{t}\) denote the respective area moments of inertia of the bottom substrate, the filler layer, and the top substrate with respect to the neutral axis and are functions of the material’s style modulus and \(L\) (see Supplementary Notes S[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#MOESM1), S[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#MOESM1)). \({E}_{b}^{ * }{i}_{b},\)\({E}^{ * }i,\) and \({E}_{t}^{ * }{i}_{t}\) represent the effective bending stiffness of the bottom substrate, the filler layer, and the top substrate, respectively. Thus, given Young’s modulus and height of the material, the device’s \({{Ei}}_{{TEG}}\) with respect to the \(F\) and \(L\) can be computed. Furthermore, as shown in Fig.[2b](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#Fig2), it is possible to derive the pressure (_Pr_) exerted on the skin as a result of the device bending against a body part (assumed to be an ideal cylinder), and this can be expressed as (see Supplementary Notes S[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#MOESM1), S[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#MOESM1)): $$Pr=\frac{12{{Ei}}_{{TEG}}}{{{W}_{{TEG}}}^{3}\left(\root 2 \of {{{r}^{2}-0.25{{W}_{{TEG}}}^{2}}}+{r}^{2}-0.25\frac{{{W}_{{TEG}}}^{2}}{r}\right)},$$ (2) where \({W}_{{TEG}}\) is the width of the TEG and \(r\) is the bending radius. Equation ([2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#Equ2)) is based on the encapsulation material being a linear elastomer. For the hyperelastomer material, we also give the derivation process (Mooney_Rivlin model, see Supplementary Note S[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-52841-1#MOESM1)), but the result is more complicated. For general applications, we can approximate the linear elastomer to simplify the analysis (It adapts to the application environment when the bending radius exceeds 5 cm.). enriched_points.expanded_content: Thermoelectric materials in wearable devices harvest body heat, enabling continuous power supply without batteries. Their flexibility and lightweight design enhance user comfort and usability.
Thermoelectric generators power remote sensors by converting ambient temperature differences into electricity, facilitating real-time data collection in inaccessible locations without maintenance.
In industrial settings, thermoelectric materials improve energy efficiency by converting waste heat from machinery into usable electricity, reducing operational costs and promoting sustainability.
